metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default timeout value (e.g., 10 s) in the plan's Security section or as an `auth_introspection_timeout` config key with a documented default, so the executor does not need to choose arbitrarily.
findings.finding: The Security section mandates a 'bounded timeout' on the introspection HTTP call but never states a default value; an executor must choose an arbitrary number without guidance.
findings.location: Security section, 'bounded timeout' constraint
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_timeout` (float, default `10.0`) to the config key list in the What section and note it in the Security section: 'httpx timeout defaults to 10 s; configurable via `auth_introspection_timeout`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the docs sub-criterion of AC1, e.g., `grep -r 'REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_' docs/` (or the equivalent docs artifact path), so the docs-regeneration requirement is verifiable in-session.
findings.finding: AC1's proving command for 'docs contain the new env-var names' is stated as a prose assertion with no concrete grep/search command, leaving that sub-criterion unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 — '(observable oracle: the manifest drift test is green and the docs contain the new env-var names)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1's observable oracle: 'and `grep -r REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_ENDPOINT docs/` exits 0 (confirming the regenerated reference docs include the new env-var names).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) with an observable oracle for the `client_id` mapping: verify that when the introspection response omits `client_id` but supplies `sub`, `AccessToken.client_id == sub`; and that when both are absent, `AccessToken.client_id == "unknown"`.
findings.finding: The What section specifies a `client_id` fallback mapping (`client_id→client_id`; absent → falls back to `sub`, else `"unknown"`), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this behavior — it is a described deliverable with no covering test oracle.
findings.location: What section (`client_id→client_id (absent → falls back to sub, else "unknown")`) vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC2–AC3 cover scopes/exp/aud/active but not client_id mapping)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 to include two sub-cases: (1) response has no `client_id` but has `sub` → `token.client_id == sub`; (2) response has neither → `token.client_id == "unknown"`. These map exactly to the already-described fallback logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Security/Cache section what the cache TTL resolves to when `exp` is absent (e.g. 'if `token_exp` is absent, cache until `now + cache_ttl` only') and add a corresponding AC assertion (e.g. extend AC3: with caching enabled, an `active:true` response omitting `exp` IS cached for the configured TTL duration).
findings.finding: The Security section's Cache formula (`min(now + cache_ttl, token_exp)`) references `token_exp`, but the Expiry sub-section explicitly states that `exp` may be absent (`expires_at=None`). The plan does not specify what cache TTL to use when `token_exp` is `None`/absent, leaving the formula underspecified for a valid code path, and no AC covers this edge case.
findings.location: Security section — 'Cache' bullet (`min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)`) vs. 'Expiry' bullet (`exp` absent → `expires_at=None`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Cache bullet: 'If `exp` is absent, the cache entry expires at `now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl` (no token-expiry bound applied).' Then add an AC3 sub-case: 'with `cache_ttl > 0`, an `active:true` response omitting `exp` → second call within TTL does NOT re-hit the fake; call after TTL does.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add inline documentation (config-file comment, reference-doc annotation, or inline docstring on the config field) for `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud` and `auth_introspection_allow_private_host` in S4 itself — do not defer all risk guidance to S7; at minimum, the regenerated reference docs for these env vars should include a one-line security note.
findings.finding: Security-sensitive config options `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud` and `auth_introspection_allow_private_host` ship in S4 with their risk documentation explicitly deferred to S7; operators enabling these flags between S4 and S7 have no inline guidance on the security tradeoff.
findings.location: Security section; AC #1; Compatibility section — 'documented risk in S7'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_config_schema.py`, annotate the two boolean fields with a docstring/comment explaining the security tradeoff (e.g., '# WARNING: setting true accepts tokens without audience binding; see S7 threat model'). In the regenerated reference docs, include a `SECURITY` note column or inline warning for these two env vars specifically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete default timeout value (e.g. 5 s) for the introspection HTTP call, state that httpx is used with `await` (async), and add a done-definition (e.g. 'auth decision completes within timeout + 50 ms processing') to the AC.
findings.finding: The plan says 'bounded timeout' but never specifies the timeout value, a latency target, or whether the httpx call is async — leaving the hot-path auth latency SLO undefined and risking a slow AS stalling every MCP request.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'The httpx introspection call uses `AsyncClient` with a default timeout of 5 s (configurable via `auth_introspection_timeout`); the unit test asserts a timeout raises and is treated as a transport error (non-acceptance).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a maximum cache-entry count (e.g. 1 000, configurable) with LRU or TTL-based eviction, and document this bound in the Security section and AC.
findings.finding: The in-process positive-response cache has no stated size bound, risking unbounded memory growth if many distinct tokens are presented (e.g., a token-per-request pattern or an attacker submitting many distinct active tokens).
findings.location: Security section — 'Cache: keyed on a hash of the token'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `cachetools.TTLCache(maxsize=auth_introspection_cache_max_size, ttl=...)` (already a common dependency) or a simple `OrderedDict` with a max-size eviction; add `auth_introspection_cache_max_size` config key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum one structured log emission per introspection failure (at WARNING level, without token/secret content), specify whether a single retry on connection-error is acceptable (or explicitly document no-retry as intentional for security), and add an AC asserting a failure is logged.
findings.finding: No retry or backoff is specified for transient AS errors, and no observability (metrics, structured log entries, or alerts) is specified for introspection failures, cache events, or AS latency — leaving operators blind to AS outages or misconfiguration.
findings.location: Security section — 'Fail-closed transport'; Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'Each transport failure emits a structured WARNING log with the endpoint URL (not the token) and error type. No automatic retry — fail-closed is intentional; operators monitor via log aggregation.' Add a corresponding AC line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'write own verifier, do not import SDK class' decision and its rationale (SDK class is an example lacking client auth, audience enforcement, and caching) in a CLAUDE.md note or inline module docstring so it is discoverable from the codebase.
findings.finding: The decision to write a custom `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` instead of importing the SDK class is a non-trivial architectural choice that is explained inline but not captured in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc — future maintainers may re-introduce an SDK import or diverge during SDK upgrades without knowing why the custom path was chosen.
findings.location: What section — 'NOTE: the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is an example, not an exported/hardened class'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `_mcp_auth.py` (or a CLAUDE.md entry) explaining that `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` intentionally does not extend/import the SDK example class and listing the hardening gaps that motivated the custom implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency safety model for the token cache: state whether a threading lock (or asyncio Lock) guards the check-then-act sequence, or declare that the plain-dict race is acceptable because cache writes are idempotent (same key → same value) and the only consequence is redundant AS calls.
findings.finding: The in-process token cache has no specified concurrency safety: concurrent requests that miss the cache simultaneously will all proceed to the introspection endpoint (benign but wasteful), and more critically, the check-then-act sequence (lookup → HTTP call → store) is an unprotected TOCTOU race that could write stale or duplicate entries under concurrent load.
findings.location: Security section — Cache subsection; AC bullet 3 (cache observable oracle)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence under the Cache security bullet: 'The cache is a dict guarded by a threading.Lock (or asyncio.Lock for async implementations); the check-look-up → HTTP call → store sequence holds the lock around the store step only (not the HTTP call) to avoid holding the lock across I/O, accepting that concurrent misses for the same token may each call the AS once (last-write-wins, both values are equivalent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the cache retirement/eviction path: specify either expiry-on-read (check timestamp on lookup, skip/delete if expired — simplest), a bounded max-size with LRU eviction, or document that the cache is intentionally unbounded and operators must bound it via `auth_introspection_cache_ttl` being short.
findings.finding: The cache eviction/retirement path is underspecified: the plan says entries are valid until min(now+ttl, token_exp) but does not state HOW expired entries are removed. Without an explicit eviction strategy (expiry-on-read, bounded LRU, or periodic sweep), a long-running server that sees many unique tokens will accumulate cache entries indefinitely, leaking memory.
findings.location: Security section — Cache subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Cache bullet: 'Expired entries are evicted lazily on read (lookup checks the stored expiry timestamp; a miss-or-expired entry is deleted and re-fetched). No background sweep is required. The cache is unbounded in size; operators with high token cardinality should keep TTL short or set TTL=0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC4 oracle for the TTL boundary: include a test case where `token_exp < now + cache_ttl` and assert the cache entry is not served after `token_exp` (e.g., mock time past `token_exp` and verify the cache misses and re-hits the fake endpoint).
findings.finding: The cache TTL boundary condition — when `token_exp < now + cache_ttl` — has no specified observable oracle in AC4, meaning an implementation that caches until `now + cache_ttl` (ignoring `token_exp`) would pass all acceptance criteria while introducing a security bug (token cached past its own expiry).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (cache behavior)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, add: 'and that when the token's own exp is sooner than now+cache_ttl, the cached entry expires at token_exp (observable oracle: advancing a mocked clock past token_exp causes a cache miss and a re-call to the fake, even when cache_ttl has not yet elapsed).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `httpx` is a declared dependency on the MCP auth code path: add `httpx` to the `mcp` optional-extra in `pyproject.toml` (or confirm a prior story has done so) — it is NOT currently declared there and the plan's 'already-present' claim is false for a `nava-rebar[mcp]`-only install.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Reuse the already-present `httpx`' (Approach & rationale section), but `httpx` is NOT a declared dependency in `pyproject.toml` for either `[project.dependencies]` or the `[project.optional-dependencies].mcp` extra. It is only available transitively via the `agents` extra (`pydantic-ai-slim` → httpx). An operator installing `nava-rebar[mcp]` — the natural install target for the MCP server — will not have httpx, causing ImportError at runtime when the introspection verifier attempts to import it.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; `pyproject.toml` `[project.optional-dependencies]`
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyproject.toml`, change `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` to `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx"]` (or add httpx to `[project.dependencies]` if the introspection verifier is in core). Also confirm S2 makes the same addition if it also uses httpx.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/config.md` to the edit-target list and include the new `mcp.auth_introspection_*` keys in the `[mcp]` section documentation there.
findings.finding: `docs/config.md` documents the `[mcp]` section config keys (currently `readonly`, `allow_llm`, `allow_jira_sync`) and is enforced by no drift gate, so adding ~6 new `auth_introspection_*` fields to `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']` will leave it stale. The plan does not list `docs/config.md` as an edit target.
findings.location: What section / AC1 — edit targets list
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/config.md` update task to the What/AC sections, documenting the 6 new `mcp.auth_introspection_*` config keys and their derived `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` env-var names alongside the existing mcp.* keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` to the edit-target list (or clarify that `docs/env-vars.md` is the intended 'reference docs') so the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` env-var names appear in the regenerated output that AC1's oracle checks.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` hardcodes only 3 gate vars (`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`) in its 'Gate environment variables' table (line 208) and is enforced by a CI drift gate. The plan's AC1 says 'reference docs are regenerated to include [the new env-var names]' — but unless `gen_mcp_reference.py` is updated to include the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` vars, regenerating it will NOT include them, and the drift gate will pass but the docs will be silent about auth config.
findings.location: AC1 — 'reference docs are regenerated to include them'; scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py line 208

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the cache-expiry formula for the `exp`-absent case: state explicitly that when `token_exp` is `None` the cache entry expires at `now + cache_ttl` (i.e., the `min()` degrades to `now + ttl` only), and add an AC-level observable oracle for this branch (e.g., 'an `active:true` response with no `exp` and `ttl>0` is cached until `now+ttl`, not rejected').
findings.finding: The Security section's cache formula `min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)` is stated as the universal rule, but `token_exp` is `None` when the introspection response omits `exp` (which the plan explicitly allows). The formula silently breaks for the `exp`-absent + caching-enabled combination: a Python `min(int, None)` raises `TypeError`. The Expiry sub-section implies the correct fallback (use `now + cache_ttl` only), but the implementation formula covering this branch is never stated.
findings.location: Security section — Cache paragraph; also Expiry sub-section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security → Cache paragraph, replace the universal formula with: "A positive is cached until `now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl` when `exp` is absent, or `min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, exp)` when `exp` is present — whichever bound is earlier." Add a corresponding AC oracle: "an `active:true` response with no `exp` field and `cache_ttl=60` is served from cache on a second call within 60 s, and the fake is not re-hit."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify against the pinned MCP SDK version (from `pyproject.toml`) that `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` is NOT a public, exported, production-grade class before committing to writing a custom implementation; document the SDK version and the verification result in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan's primary justification for writing a custom verifier — 'the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is an example, not an exported/hardened class' — is an unverified capability assertion about the installed MCP SDK version. The symbol does not resolve cleanly from the installed package, but `UNRESOLVED` is not proof of absence; the SDK is actively developed and may have promoted this class to a public API by the time of implementation.
findings.location: What section: 'NOTE: the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is an example, not an exported/hardened class — this story WRITES its own'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Verified against mcp=={pinned_version}: `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` lives only under `examples/` and is not importable from any public `mcp.*` module path (checked `mcp.server.auth`, `mcp.server.auth.middleware.bearer_auth`). If a future SDK upgrade promotes it, revisit S4.' This makes the prior-art decision auditable and version-pinned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `mcp.server.auth.provider.AccessToken` exposes a `resource` field (inspect the dataclass definition or SDK changelog for mcp>=1.9) and document the exact field name/type in the plan; if the field is absent or differently named, update the mapping accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the verifier 'stamps resource=auth_resource_server_url so the composite check passes,' which requires mcp.server.auth.provider.AccessToken to have a field named `resource`. AccessToken resolves in the installed SDK (Partially-verified), but the specific `resource` field on that dataclass cannot be confirmed from available tooling. If the field is absent or differently named (e.g. the SDK uses `aud` or omits it), the composite-check integration claim silently breaks at runtime.
findings.location: What section: 'stamps resource=auth_resource_server_url so the composite check passes'; AC-1 (AccessToken mapping)
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What section, add: 'Verified against mcp>=1.9: AccessToken has fields [list them]. The `resource` field is set via [constructor kwarg / direct assignment / builder method].' If introspection by schema is not feasible pre-implementation, add an AC assertion: 'unit test asserts that constructing an AccessToken with resource=auth_resource_server_url succeeds and the field round-trips correctly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare that a missing or empty value for the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` causes a fail-closed construction-time error (raise `ConfigError` or equivalent), and add an AC4 oracle asserting this rejection.
findings.finding: The plan's secret-resolution path for `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` does not declare behavior when the named env var is absent or empty at runtime; a silent empty-string resolution would send the introspection request with an empty Basic-auth password, which some Authorization Servers accept, potentially bypassing client authentication on the boundary-crossing call.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env'; What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'If the env var named by auth_introspection_client_secret_env is absent or empty at construction time, the verifier raises ConfigError — no unauthenticated introspection call is ever made.' Mirror this as an observable oracle in AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete default timeout value (e.g. 10 s) to the plan — either as a named `auth_introspection_timeout` config key with a documented default, or as an explicit constant in the Security section — and add an AC oracle asserting the httpx call uses that bounded timeout.
findings.finding: The Security section mandates a 'bounded timeout' on the introspection HTTP call but specifies no default value; on the boundary-crossing auth path, an executor-chosen large timeout (e.g. 300 s) allows a lower-trust actor who can influence AS response latency to hold the auth decision open indefinitely, constituting a denial-of-service on the authentication path.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; What section — config keys list (no auth_introspection_timeout key present)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_timeout` (float, default `10.0`, minimum `0.1`) to the config key list in the What section and note in the Security section: 'httpx timeout defaults to 10 s (auth_introspection_timeout); a hung AS is treated as a transport error (raises, not cached).' Add to AC4: 'observable oracle: the httpx client is constructed with a timeout ≤ auth_introspection_timeout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full valid value vocabulary for `mcp.auth_strategies` (the closed set of accepted strategy strings, e.g. `{"introspection"}` for S4's scope or a broader set if S2 already defines others) and co-locate it with the `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer specification so the coercer can validate the list entries with `_as_choice` or equivalent.
findings.finding: The plan activates the verifier by including `introspection` in `mcp.auth_strategies` — a net-new config list key described as 'introduced by S2' — but the full valid value vocabulary for this key is never specified anywhere in the plan, and it has zero occurrences in the codebase. An executor writing the `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer for `auth_strategies` cannot know what other strategy strings are valid (e.g., `jwt`, `jwks`, `none`), whether the list must be non-empty, whether order matters, or what invalid values should be rejected with.
findings.location: What section — 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key introduced by S2)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'The `_SECTIONS["mcp"]` coercer for `auth_strategies` accepts a list (or comma-separated string) whose elements must each be in the closed set `{"introspection"}` (the only S4-introduced value; S2 may have added others — enumerate them here). The coercer calls `_as_str_tuple` then validates each element against the closed set, raising `ConfigError` on unknown values.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `exp`-absent caching fallback rule with the cache formula in the Security/Cache paragraph: add an explicit statement such as 'when `exp` is absent, `token_exp` is treated as `+∞` (i.e., cache until `now + cache_ttl` only; the `min` collapses to `now + cache_ttl`)' so the two sub-sections are self-consistent and no cross-inference is required.
findings.finding: The cache processing protocol states `min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)` as the universal caching rule, but the plan also explicitly states `exp` may be absent (→ `expires_at=None`). The branch `caching_enabled AND exp_absent` is never given an explicit formula or decision rule — the fallback must be inferred by cross-reading two separate non-adjacent sub-sections (Security/Cache and Security/Expiry). This is a structural gap: the decision rule for the incomplete sub-step (absent `exp` when cache TTL > 0) is not co-located with the cache algorithm.
findings.location: Security section — 'Cache' paragraph (`min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)`) and 'Expiry' paragraph (`When active:true but exp is absent … set expires_at=None`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Cache paragraph, append: 'When the response omits `exp` (`token_exp` is absent), the formula simplifies to `now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl` (no expiry bound from the token itself). The `min` is only applied when `token_exp` is a real positive value.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the regenerated `docs/mcp-reference.md`/`docs/env-vars.md` pick these up via S1's fixes making `gen_mcp_reference.py`/`gen_env_registry.py` iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`.' Reading both scripts refutes this: `gen_mcp_reference.py` renders exactly 3 hardcoded gate-var names from `MCP_ENV_VARS` (line 208: `for var in ('REBAR_MCP_READONLY', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC')`), not a full iteration; `gen_env_registry.py` discovers vars via AST source scanning of `os.environ` literal reads, not via `MCP_ENV_VARS`. If this is what S1 is supposed to fix, S4 depends on S1 delivering changes that are not yet present — and if those changes are not delivered, registering auth vars in `MCP_ENV_VARS` will not cause them to appear in either generated doc.
findings.location: What section ('the regenerated `docs/mcp-reference.md`/`docs/env-vars.md` pick these up via S1's fixes...') and AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit AC sub-bullet that verifies S1's generator changes are in place before S4 lands, or move the generator-fix work into S4's scope with a concrete description of what each script change must do (e.g., gen_env_registry.py must union in MCP_ENV_VARS entries; gen_mcp_reference.py must iterate all MCP_ENV_VARS in its env-var table rather than the hardcoded 3-var list).

findings.finding: The plan asserts that registering the `auth_introspection_*` keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS` and running `gen_mcp_reference.py` will cause those keys to 'appear in the regenerated reference docs', but the generator's gate-variable table is hardcoded to exactly three vars (`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`). New keys added to `MCP_ENV_VARS` are NOT automatically surfaced in the rendered doc — the script would also need to be updated, but the plan does not mention updating `gen_mcp_reference.py`.
findings.location: What section / AC line 1: 'Registers its keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` + regenerates the reference docs'
findings.suggested_fix: In `gen_mcp_reference.py`, replace the hardcoded 3-name loop in `render()` with an iteration over all entries in `MCP_ENV_VARS` (or a filtered subset), so any new keys added to the canonical list are automatically included in the rendered gate-variables table.

findings.finding: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (`McpConfig` dataclass and its coercion tables `_SECTION_CLASSES`/`_SECTIONS`) is an unenumerated edit target: the plan's `auth_introspection_*` flat config keys (which the plan says live under `mcp.auth_*` TOML shape) require new fields on `McpConfig` and new coercion entries — but the plan names only `_mcp_auth.py`, `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, and the doc generators as edit targets.
findings.location: What section ('flat config keys THIS story registers') / Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_endpoint: str = ''`, `auth_introspection_client_id: str = ''`, etc. as fields on `McpConfig`, and register their coercers in `_SECTIONS['mcp']`.

findings.finding: The plan conflates TOML config keys (`auth_introspection_endpoint`, etc.) with `MCP_ENV_VARS` entries (which currently are environment variable names like `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`). Registering TOML config keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS` / `server.json` is architecturally inconsistent with how the existing config surface works, and no translation layer (env-var names vs. TOML keys) is specified.
findings.location: What section ('flat config keys THIS story registers … Registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json')
findings.suggested_fix: Only `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` (the env-var name indirection) and possibly the secret's own env var name belong in MCP_ENV_VARS; endpoint/ttl/allow_* are TOML config keys for McpConfig.

findings.finding: The cache-expiry semantic — that a cached positive is evicted at `min(now + cache_ttl, token_exp)` and a subsequent call re-hits the introspection endpoint — has no observable oracle in AC4. The two oracles listed cover only cache-key format, not post-TTL re-introspection. Without an explicit assertion that the verifier re-contacts the AS after the cache entry expires, the critical security property (caching does not extend token validity beyond configured TTL or token exp) is untestable from the ACs as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (cache behavior block)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4 with: 'and that after the cache entry expires (tested via time-mocking for both the cache_ttl-bound case and the token_exp-bound case), a subsequent verification call re-hits the fake endpoint — confirming the eviction bound is min(now + cache_ttl, token_exp) and not either bound alone.'

findings.finding: `gen_env_registry.py` is a pure AST scanner of `src/rebar` source files and does NOT currently iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`; the plan's claim that S1's fixes make it iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS` implies a significant refactor of that script, but S1 is not listed in the Scope's dependency list and no edit to `gen_env_registry.py` is enumerated in S4's own edit set.
findings.location: What section / AC line 1 ('via S1's fixes making gen_env_registry.py iterate MCP_ENV_VARS')

findings.finding: The What section states the verifier 'maps `scope`/`exp`/`sub`/`client_id` into an `AccessToken`', naming four response fields as deliverables. However, neither the plan nor any AC specifies which `AccessToken` fields receive `sub` and `client_id` (the SDK's `AccessToken` schema is not reproduced or cited). Only `scopes`, `resource`, and `expires_at` are oracle'd in AC-2; `sub` and `client_id` mapping have no verification path, making them untestable named deliverables.
findings.location: What section — 'maps `scope`/`exp`/`sub`/`client_id` into an `AccessToken`'; AC-2 (unit test assertions)

findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at runtime — fail at construction, fail at first call, or treat as unauthenticated. This is a security-relevant defaultable gap: silent fallback to no client auth would bypass the authentication requirement.
findings.location: What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section or What section, add: 'If the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ValueError` (fail-closed, same guard family as http:// and private-host checks).' Then add this case to AC5's observable oracle.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the failure mode when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at runtime. An unset env var could result in an unauthenticated introspection call (no Authorization header) that some permissive Authorization Servers will answer — silently accepting every token without client authentication.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic (secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env)'; AC — 'Unit test asserts construction-time guards: the introspection request carries client_secret_basic (secret sourced from auth_introspection_client_secret_env)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'If the env var named by auth_introspection_client_secret_env is absent or empty at construction, IntrospectionTokenVerifier raises ConfigError — it never starts without client credentials.' Add to the construction-time-guards AC: 'and that constructing the verifier with an unset/empty client secret env var raises at construction.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that the client secret is sourced from the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`, but does not declare what happens when that env var is absent or empty at startup — whether construction fails (fail-closed) or the verifier proceeds without client authentication (fail-open). An unauthenticated introspection call would violate RFC 7662 §2.1 and the plan's own 'authenticated TLS' requirement, and could allow any bearer to pass validation if the AS accepts unauthenticated introspection.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env'; What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'If the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty, construction MUST raise `ConfigError` (fail-closed).' Add a unit-test AC bullet for this case.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
